and sometimes referred to as **anti-terrorism**, encompasses the laws, policies, and operational measures used to prevent, deter, and respond to [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism"). These efforts involve a range of tactics and strategies employed by governments, [law-enforcement](/wiki/Law-enforcement "Law-enforcement") agencies, [intelligence services](/wiki/Intelligence_agency "Intelligence agency") and, in some contexts, private-sector organisations.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/PI2G_2F.jpg/250px-PI2G_2F.jpg)](/wiki/File:PI2G_2F.jpg)

[GIGN](/wiki/GIGN "GIGN") operators in 2015. GIGN is the counterterrorist [tactical unit](/wiki/Police_tactical_unit "Police tactical unit") of the [National Gendarmerie](/wiki/National_Gendarmerie "National Gendarmerie") of France.

**Counterterrorism**, also spelled **counter-terrorism** and sometimes referred to as **anti-terrorism**, encompasses the laws, policies, and operational measures used to prevent, deter, and respond to [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism"). These efforts involve a range of tactics and strategies employed by governments, [law-enforcement](/wiki/Law-enforcement "Law-enforcement") agencies, [intelligence services](/wiki/Intelligence_agency "Intelligence agency") and, in some contexts, private-sector organisations.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

When acts of terrorism occur within a broader [insurgency](/wiki/Insurgency "Insurgency")—an activity that some [definitions of terrorism](/wiki/Definitions_of_terrorism "Definitions of terrorism") include—counterterrorism efforts may incorporate [counterinsurgency](/wiki/Counterinsurgency "Counterinsurgency") measures. In United States [military doctrine](/wiki/Military_doctrine "Military doctrine"), such activities are often framed as *[foreign internal defense](/wiki/Foreign_internal_defense "Foreign internal defense")*, a term referring to programs that assist partner nations in suppressing insurgency, [lawlessness](/wiki/Anarchy "Anarchy"), or [subversion](/wiki/Subversion "Subversion"), and in reducing conditions that could give rise to threats to [national security](/wiki/National_security "National security").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The first dedicated counterterrorism unit was the [Special Irish Branch](/wiki/Special_Branch_(Metropolitan_Police) "Special Branch (Metropolitan Police)") of the [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police"), established in 1883 to counter the [Fenian](/wiki/Fenian "Fenian") bombing campaign. It was later renamed the Special Branch as its remit expanded beyond [Irish republican](/wiki/Irish_republicanism "Irish republicanism") activity. Police forces across the United Kingdom and in other countries subsequently created similar units.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The [International Conference of Rome for the Social Defense Against Anarchists](/wiki/International_Conference_of_Rome_for_the_Social_Defense_Against_Anarchists "International Conference of Rome for the Social Defense Against Anarchists"), held in 1898, is widely regarded as the first international conference convened to address terrorism, bringing together 54 delegates from 21 countries to coordinate measures against anarchist violence.[[6]](#cite_note-t136-6)

The first modern police tactical counterterrorism unit is widely considered to be [GSG 9](/wiki/GSG_9 "GSG 9"), established within [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany")'s Federal Border Guard (**[Bundesgrenzschutz](/wiki/Bundesgrenzschutz "Bundesgrenzschutz")**), on 26 September 1972 in response to the [Munich Olympic massacre](/wiki/Munich_Olympic_massacre "Munich Olympic massacre"). The Bundesgrenzschutz was later reorganised as the [Federal Police](/wiki/Federal_Police_(Germany) "Federal Police (Germany)") (**Bundespolizei**) in 2005, with GSG 9 continuing as its elite counterterrorism and hostage‑rescue unit.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Counterterrorist capabilities expanded during the late twentieth century as governments responded to a perceived rise in terrorist activity. After the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") in 2001, Western governments made counterterrorism a central national‑security priority, introducing major institutional reforms, increasing international cooperation, and adopting new operational approaches such as [red team](/wiki/Red_team "Red team")[[8]](#cite_note-8) analysis and a wider range of preventive measures.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Although terrorist attacks in Western countries tend to receive disproportionately high levels of media coverage,[[10]](#cite_note-10) most terrorist incidents occur in less developed regions.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Scholars have noted that government responses to terrorism can sometimes produce unintended consequences, including operational failures or escalation of violence.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The failed rescue attempt during the Munich massacre is a widely cited example, prompting major reforms in crisis‑response capabilities.

## Planning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Planning")]

### Intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance")]

See also: [Intelligence cycle management](/wiki/Intelligence_cycle_management "Intelligence cycle management"), [Intelligence analysis](/wiki/Intelligence_analysis "Intelligence analysis"), [HUMINT](/wiki/HUMINT "HUMINT"), and [Counterintelligence](/wiki/Counterintelligence "Counterintelligence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Magav-Facebook--Yamam-0009.jpg/330px-Magav-Facebook--Yamam-0009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Magav-Facebook--Yamam-0009.jpg)

[Yamam](/wiki/Yamam "Yamam"), one of Israel's counterterrorism units

Most counterterrorism strategies involve an increase in policing and domestic [intelligence](/wiki/Military_intelligence "Military intelligence") gathering. Central techniques include [intercepting communications](/wiki/Wiretapping "Wiretapping") and [location tracking](/wiki/Tracking_system "Tracking system"). New technology has expanded the range of [military](/wiki/Manhunt_(military) "Manhunt (military)") and [law enforcement](/wiki/Manhunt_(law_enforcement) "Manhunt (law enforcement)") options for intelligence gathering. Many countries increasingly employ [facial recognition systems](/wiki/Facial_recognition_system "Facial recognition system") in policing.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Domestic intelligence gathering is sometimes directed to specific [ethnic](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity") or religious groups, which are the sources of political controversy. [Mass surveillance](/wiki/Mass_surveillance "Mass surveillance") of an entire population raises objections on [civil liberties](/wiki/Civil_liberties "Civil liberties") grounds. [Domestic terrorists](/wiki/Domestic_terrorism "Domestic terrorism"), especially [lone wolves](/wiki/Lone_wolf_attack "Lone wolf attack"), are often harder to detect because of their citizenship or legal status and ability to stay under the radar.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

To select the effective action when terrorism appears to be more of an isolated event, the appropriate government organizations need to understand the source, motivation, methods of preparation, and tactics of terrorist groups. Good intelligence is at the heart of such preparation, as well as a political and social understanding of any grievances that might be solved. Ideally, one gets information from inside the group, a very difficult challenge for [human intelligence](/wiki/Human_intelligence_(intelligence_gathering) "Human intelligence (intelligence gathering)") operations because [operational terrorist cells](/wiki/Clandestine_cell_system "Clandestine cell system") are often small, with all members known to one another, perhaps even related.[[17]](#cite_note-Feiler2007-17)

[Counterintelligence](/wiki/Counterintelligence "Counterintelligence") is a great challenge with the security of cell-based systems, since the ideal, but the nearly impossible, goal is to obtain a [clandestine source](/wiki/Clandestine_human_intelligence "Clandestine human intelligence") within the cell. Financial tracking can play a role, as a [communications intercept](/wiki/Signals_intelligence "Signals intelligence"). However, both of these approaches need to be balanced against legitimate expectations of privacy.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Legal contexts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Legal contexts")]

Main article: [Anti-terrorism legislation](/wiki/Anti-terrorism_legislation "Anti-terrorism legislation")

In response to the growing legislation.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom_%281-2%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom_%281-2%29.svg.png) [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")

* The United Kingdom has had counterterrorism legislation in place for more than thirty years. The Prevention of Violence Act 1938 was brought in response to an [Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army "Irish Republican Army") (IRA) campaign of violence under the [S-Plan](/wiki/S-Plan "S-Plan"). This act had been allowed to expire in 1953. It was repealed in 1973 to be replaced by the [Prevention of Terrorism Acts](/wiki/Prevention_of_Terrorism_Acts "Prevention of Terrorism Acts"), a response to [the Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles") in Northern Ireland. From 1974 to 1989, the temporary provisions of the act were renewed annually. Their original counterterrorism legislation was used to predict future IRA attacks and respond accordingly based on their predictions. For example, if British Security Forces attacked, they predicted the IRA would target civilian areas as a response.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* In 2000 the Acts were replaced with the more permanent [Terrorism Act 2000](/wiki/Terrorism_Act_2000 "Terrorism Act 2000"), which contained many of their powers, and then the [Prevention of Terrorism Act 2005](/wiki/Prevention_of_Terrorism_Act_2005 "Prevention of Terrorism Act 2005").
* The [Anti-terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001](/wiki/Anti-terrorism,_Crime_and_Security_Act_2001 "Anti-terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001") was formally introduced into the Parliament on November 19, 2001, two months after the [September 11, 2001 attacks](/wiki/September_11,_2001_attacks "September 11, 2001 attacks") in the United States. It received royal assent and went into force on December 13, 2001. On December 16, 2004, the Law Lords ruled that Part 4 was incompatible with the European Convention on Human Rights. However, under the terms of the [Human Rights Act 1998](/wiki/Human_Rights_Act_1998 "Human Rights Act 1998"), it remained in force. The [Prevention of Terrorism Act 2005](/wiki/Prevention_of_Terrorism_Act_2005 "Prevention of Terrorism Act 2005") was drafted to answer the Law Lords ruling and the [Terrorism Act 2006](/wiki/Terrorism_Act_2006 "Terrorism Act 2006") creates new offenses related to terrorism, and amends existing ones. The act was drafted in the aftermath of the [7 July 2005 London bombings](/wiki/7_July_2005_London_bombings "7 July 2005 London bombings") and, like its predecessors, some of its terms have proven to be highly controversial.

Since 1978 the UK's terrorism laws have been regularly reviewed by a security-cleared [Independent Reviewer of Terrorism Legislation](/wiki/Independent_Reviewer_of_Terrorism_Legislation "Independent Reviewer of Terrorism Legislation"), whose often influential reports are submitted to Parliament and published in full.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/5d/Flag_of_the_United_States_%2823px%29.png) [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")

* U.S. legal issues surrounding this issue include rulings on the domestic employment of [deadly force](/wiki/Deadly_force "Deadly force") by law enforcement agencies.
* Search and seizure is governed by the [Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution](/wiki/Fourth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution").
* The U.S. passed the [Patriot Act](/wiki/Patriot_Act "Patriot Act") after the September 11 attacks, as well as a range of other legislation and [executive orders](/wiki/Executive_orders "Executive orders") relating to national security.
* The [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security") was established to consolidate domestic security agencies to coordinate counterterrorism and national response to major natural disasters and accidents.
* The [United States Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard")'s mission, only military branch with law enforcement jurisdiction to board and seize *"The direct or indirect supply, sale, or transfer of weapons to terrorist organizations violates U.N. Security Resolution 2216 (as extended and renewed by resolutions 2675 and 2707) and international law."*[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* The [Posse Comitatus Act](/wiki/Posse_Comitatus_Act "Posse Comitatus Act") limits domestic employment of the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") and the [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force"), requiring Presidential approval before deploying the Army or the Air Force. [Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") policy also applies this limitation to the [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") and the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy"), because the Posse Comitatus Act does not cover naval services, even though they are federal military forces. The Department of Defense can be employed domestically on Presidential order, as was done during the [Los Angeles riots of 1992](/wiki/Los_Angeles_riots_of_1992 "Los Angeles riots of 1992"), [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina"), and the [Beltway Sniper](/wiki/Beltway_Sniper "Beltway Sniper") incidents.
* External or international use of lethal force would require a [Presidential finding](/wiki/Presidential_finding "Presidential finding").
* In February 2017, sources claimed that the Trump administration intends to rename and revamp the U.S. government program [Countering Violent Extremism](/wiki/Countering_Violent_Extremism_Task_Force "Countering Violent Extremism Task Force") (CVE) to focus solely on Islamist extremism.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Flag_of_Australia_%28converted%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_Australia_%28converted%29.svg.png) [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia")

* Australia has passed several counterterrorism acts. In 2004, a bill comprising three acts [Anti-terrorism Act, 2004, (No 2) and (No 3)](/wiki/Australian_anti-terrorism_legislation,_2004 "Australian anti-terrorism legislation, 2004") was passed. Then Attorney-General, [Philip Ruddock](/wiki/Philip_Ruddock "Philip Ruddock"), introduced the *Anti-terrorism bill, 2004* on March 31. He described it as "a bill to strengthen Australia's counter-terrorism laws in a number of respects – a task made more urgent following the recent tragic [terrorist bombings](/wiki/2004_Madrid_train_bombings "2004 Madrid train bombings") in Spain." He said that Australia's counterterrorism laws "require review and, where necessary, updating if we are to have a legal framework capable of safeguarding all Australians from the scourge of terrorism." The [Australian Anti-Terrorism Act 2005](/wiki/Australian_Anti-Terrorism_Act_2005 "Australian Anti-Terrorism Act 2005") supplemented the powers of the earlier acts. The Australian legislation allows police to detain suspects for up to two weeks without charge and to electronically track suspects for up to a year. The [Australian Anti-Terrorism Act of 2005](/wiki/Australian_Anti-Terrorism_Act_2005#The_"Shoot_to_Kill"_Clause "Australian Anti-Terrorism Act 2005") included a "shoot-to-kill" clause. In a country with entrenched [liberal democratic](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") traditions, the measures are controversial and have been criticized by [civil libertarians](/wiki/Civil_libertarian "Civil libertarian") and [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") groups.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Flag_of_Israel.svg/40px-Flag_of_Israel.svg.png) [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel")

* Israel monitors a list of designated [terrorist organizations](/wiki/Terrorist_organization "Terrorist organization") and has laws forbidding membership in such organizations and funding or helping them.
* On December 14, 2006, the [Israeli Supreme Court](/wiki/Israeli_Supreme_Court "Israeli Supreme Court") ruled [targeted killings](/wiki/Israeli_targeted_killings "Israeli targeted killings") were a permitted form of self-defense.[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* In 2016 the Israeli [Knesset](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset") passed a comprehensive law against terrorism, forbidding any kind of terrorism and support of terrorism, and setting severe punishments for terrorists. The law also regulates legal efforts against terrorism.[[24]](#cite_note-Israel-ani-terror-law-24)

### Human rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Human rights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/John_Walker_Lindh_Custody.jpg/250px-John_Walker_Lindh_Custody.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Walker_Lindh_Custody.jpg)

[John Walker Lindh](/wiki/John_Walker_Lindh "John Walker Lindh") was captured as an enemy combatant during the United States' [2001 invasion of Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)").

One of the primary difficulties of implementing effective counterterrorist measures is the waning of civil liberties and individual privacy that such measures often entail, both for citizens of, and for those detained by states attempting to combat terror.[[25]](#cite_note-25) At times, measures designed to tighten security have been seen as [abuses of power](/wiki/Abuses_of_power "Abuses of power") or even violations of human rights.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Examples of these problems can include prolonged, incommunicado detention without judicial review or long periods of 'preventive detention';[[27]](#cite_note-27) risk of subjecting to torture during the transfer, return and extradition of people between or within countries; and the adoption of security measures that restrain the rights or freedoms of citizens and breach principles of non-discrimination.[[28]](#cite_note-Human_Rights_News_2004-28) Examples include:

* In November 2003, [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") passed new counterterrorism laws, widely criticized by local human rights groups for being vague and overbroad. Critics claim that the laws put the fundamental rights of free expression, association, and assembly at risk. Malaysia persisted in holding around 100 alleged militants without trial, including five Malaysian students detained for alleged terrorist activity while studying in Karachi, Pakistan.[[28]](#cite_note-Human_Rights_News_2004-28)
* In November 2003, a Canadian-Syrian national, Maher Arar, publicly alleged that he had been tortured in a Syrian prison after being handed over to the Syrian authorities by the U.S.[[28]](#cite_note-Human_Rights_News_2004-28)
* In December 2003, Colombia's congress approved legislation that would give the military the power to arrest, tap telephones, and carry out searches without warrants or any previous judicial order.[[28]](#cite_note-Human_Rights_News_2004-28)
* Images of torture and ill-treatment of detainees in U.S. custody in Iraq and other locations have encouraged international scrutiny of U.S. operations in the war on terror.[[29]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International_2005-29)
* Hundreds of foreign nationals remain in prolonged indefinite detention without charge or trial in Guantánamo Bay, despite international and U.S. constitutional standards some groups believe outlaw such practices.[[29]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International_2005-29)
* Hundreds of people suspected of connections with the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban") or [Al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") remain in long-term detention in Pakistan or in U.S.-controlled centers in Afghanistan without trial.[[29]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International_2005-29)
* China has used the "war on terror" to justify its policies in the predominantly Muslim [Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region](/wiki/Xinjiang_Uyghur_Autonomous_Region "Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region") to stifle Uyghur identity.[[29]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International_2005-29)
* In Morocco, Saudi Arabia, Tunisia, Yemen, and other countries, scores of people have been arrested and arbitrarily detained in connection with suspected terrorist acts or links to opposition armed groups.[[29]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International_2005-29)
* Until 2005, eleven men remained in high security detention in the UK under the Anti-Terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001.[[29]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International_2005-29)
* United Nations experts condemned the misuse of counterterrorism powers by the Egyptian authorities following the arrest, detention, and designation of human rights activists Ramy Shaath and Zyad El-Elaimy as terrorists. The two activists were arrested in June 2019, and the first-ever renewal of remand detention for Shaath came for the 21st time in 19 months on 24 January 2021. Experts called it alarming, and demanded the urgent implementation of the Working Group's opinion and removal of the two's names from the "terrorism entities' list.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Many argue that such violations of rights could exacerbate rather than counter the terrorist threat.[[28]](#cite_note-Human_Rights_News_2004-28) Human rights activists argue for the crucial role of human rights protection as an intrinsic part to fight against terrorism.[[29]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International_2005-29)[[31]](#cite_note-31) This suggests, as proponents of [human security](/wiki/Human_security "Human security") have long argued, that respecting human rights may indeed help us to incur security. [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") included a section on confronting terrorism in the recommendations in the Madrid Agenda arising from the Madrid Summit on Democracy and Terrorism (Madrid March 8–11, 2005):

> Democratic principles and values are essential tools in the fight against terrorism. Any successful strategy for dealing with terrorism requires terrorists to be isolated. Consequently, the preference must be to treat terrorism as criminal acts to be handled through existing systems of law enforcement and with full respect for human rights and the rule of law. We recommend: (1) taking effective measures to make impunity impossible either for acts of terrorism or for the abuse of human rights in counter-terrorism measures. (2) the incorporation of human rights laws in all anti-terrorism programs and policies of national governments as well as international bodies."[[29]](#cite_note-Amnesty_International_2005-29)

While international efforts to combat terrorism have focused on the need to enhance cooperation between states, proponents of human rights (as well as [human security](/wiki/Human_security "Human security")) have suggested that more effort needs to be given to the effective inclusion of human rights protection as a crucial element in that cooperation. They argue that international human rights obligations do not stop at borders, and a failure to respect human rights in one state may undermine its effectiveness in the global effort to cooperate to combat terrorism.[[28]](#cite_note-Human_Rights_News_2004-28)

### Preemptive neutralization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Preemptive neutralization")]

Some countries see preemptive attacks as a legitimate strategy. This includes capturing, killing, or disabling suspected terrorists before they can mount an attack. Israel, the United Kingdom, the United States, and Russia have taken this approach, while Western European states generally do not.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Another major method of preemptive neutralization is the [interrogation](/wiki/Interrogation "Interrogation") of known or suspected terrorists to obtain information about specific plots, targets, the identity of other terrorists, whether or not the interrogation subjects himself is guilty of terrorist involvement. Sometimes more extreme methods are used to increase [suggestibility](/wiki/Suggestibility "Suggestibility"), such as [sleep deprivation](/wiki/Sleep_deprivation "Sleep deprivation") or drugs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Such methods may lead captives to offer false information in an attempt to stop the treatment, or due to the confusion caused by it.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1978 the [European Court of Human Rights](/wiki/European_Court_of_Human_Rights "European Court of Human Rights") ruled in the [Ireland v. United Kingdom](/wiki/Ireland_v._United_Kingdom_(1978) "Ireland v. United Kingdom (1978)") case that [such methods](/wiki/Five_techniques "Five techniques") amounted to a practice of [inhuman and degrading treatment](/wiki/Inhuman_or_degrading_treatment "Inhuman or degrading treatment"), and that such practices were in breach of the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights") Article 3 (art. 3).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Non-military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Non-military")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Transparent_garbage_bins_at_Central_station.jpg/250px-Transparent_garbage_bins_at_Central_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Transparent_garbage_bins_at_Central_station.jpg)

Transparent [garbage bin](/wiki/Waste_container "Waste container") installed at [Central Station](/wiki/Central_railway_station,_Sydney "Central railway station, Sydney") in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), Australia. The bin's transparency allows police to easily examine its contents, discouraging the placement of bombs or weaponry inside.

The [human security](/wiki/Human_security "Human security") paradigm outlines a non-military approach that aims to address the enduring underlying inequalities which fuel terrorist activity. Causal factors need to be delineated and measures implemented which allow equal access to resources and [sustainability](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") for all people. Such activities empower citizens, providing "freedom from fear" and "freedom from want".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This can take many forms, including the provision of clean drinking water, education, vaccination programs, provision of food and shelter and protection from violence, military or otherwise. Successful human security campaigns have been characterized by the participation of a diverse group of actors, including governments, [NGOs](/wiki/NGOs "NGOs"), and citizens.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Foreign internal defense](/wiki/Foreign_internal_defense "Foreign internal defense") programs provide outside expert assistance to a threatened government. FID can involve both non-military and military aspects of counterterrorism.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A 2017 study found that "governance and civil society aid is effective in dampening domestic terrorism, but this effect is only present if the recipient country is not experiencing a civil conflict."[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Further information: [Violent extremism § Prevention of radicalization and deradicalization](/wiki/Violent_extremism#Prevention_of_radicalization_and_deradicalization "Violent extremism")

#### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Military")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/3RAR_Afghanistan_patrol_Aug_08.jpg/250px-3RAR_Afghanistan_patrol_Aug_08.jpg)](/wiki/File:3RAR_Afghanistan_patrol_Aug_08.jpg)

[Australian Army](/wiki/Australian_Army "Australian Army") soldiers under the [International Security Assistance Force](/wiki/International_Security_Assistance_Force "International Security Assistance Force") interacting with a civilian in [Tarinkot](/wiki/Tarinkot "Tarinkot"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") in 2008 during the [War in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)"), part of the [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror")

Terrorism has often been used to justify [military intervention](/wiki/Military_intervention "Military intervention") in countries where terrorists are said to be based. Similar justifications were used for the [U.S. invasion of Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)") and the [second Russian invasion of Chechnya](/wiki/Second_Chechen_War "Second Chechen War").

Military intervention has not always been successful in stopping or preventing future terrorism, such as during the [Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency"), the [Mau Mau uprising](/wiki/Mau_Mau_uprising "Mau Mau uprising"), and most of the campaigns against the [IRA](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army "Irish Republican Army") during the [Irish Civil War](/wiki/Irish_Civil_War "Irish Civil War"), the [S-Plan](/wiki/S-Plan "S-Plan"), the [Border Campaign](/wiki/Border_campaign_(Irish_Republican_Army) "Border campaign (Irish Republican Army)"), and [the Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles") in Northern Ireland. Although military action can temporarily disrupt a terrorist group's operations temporarily, it sometimes does not end the threat completely.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Repression by the military in itself usually leads to short term victories, but tend to be unsuccessful in the long run (e.g., the French doctrine used in colonial [Indochina](/wiki/Mainland_Southeast_Asia "Mainland Southeast Asia") and [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria")[[34]](#cite_note-Trinquier-34)), particularly if it is not accompanied by other measures. However, new methods such as those taken in [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") have yet to be seen as beneficial or ineffectual.[[35]](#cite_note-FM3-24-35)

## Preparation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Preparation")]

### Target hardening

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Target hardening")]

Whatever the target of terrorists, there are multiple ways of hardening the targets to prevent the terrorists from hitting their mark, or reducing the damage of attacks. One method is to place [hostile vehicle mitigation](/wiki/Hostile_vehicle_mitigation "Hostile vehicle mitigation") to enforce protective standoff distance outside tall or politically sensitive buildings to prevent [car bombings](/wiki/Car_bomb "Car bomb"). Another way to reduce the impact of attacks is to design buildings for rapid evacuation.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Aircraft cockpits are kept locked during flights and have reinforced doors, which only the pilots in the cabin are capable of opening. UK [railway stations](/wiki/Railway_stations "Railway stations") removed their garbage bins in response to the [Provisional IRA](/wiki/Provisional_Irish_Republican_Army "Provisional Irish Republican Army") threat, as convenient locations for depositing bombs. Scottish stations removed theirs after the [7 July 2005 London Bombings](/wiki/7_July_2005_London_Bombings "7 July 2005 London Bombings") as a precautionary measure. The [Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Transportation_Authority "Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority") purchased bomb-resistant barriers after the September 11 attacks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Operation_Guardian_of_the_Walls%2C_May_2021._XVIII.jpeg/250px-Operation_Guardian_of_the_Walls%2C_May_2021._XVIII.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Operation_Guardian_of_the_Walls,_May_2021._XVIII.jpeg)

Israeli [Iron Dome](/wiki/Iron_Dome "Iron Dome") active protection system, capable of intercepting airborne rockets and artillery shells

Due to frequent shelling of Israel's cities, towns, and settlements by [artillery rockets](/wiki/Artillery_rocket "Artillery rocket") from the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") (mainly by [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas"), but also by other Palestinian factions) and [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") (mainly by [Hezbollah](/wiki/Hezbollah "Hezbollah")), Israel has developed several defensive measures against artillery, rockets, and missiles. These include building a [bomb shelter](/wiki/Bomb_shelter "Bomb shelter") in every building and school, but also deploying [active protection systems](/wiki/Active_protection_system "Active protection system") such as the [Arrow ABM](/wiki/Arrow_(missile_family) "Arrow (missile family)"), [Iron Dome](/wiki/Iron_Dome "Iron Dome") and [David's Sling](/wiki/David%27s_Sling "David's Sling"), which intercept the incoming threat in the air. [Iron Dome](/wiki/Iron_Dome "Iron Dome") has successfully intercepted hundreds of [Qassam rockets](/wiki/Qassam_rocket "Qassam rocket") and [Grad rockets](/wiki/BM-21_Grad "BM-21 Grad") fired by Palestinians from the Gaza Strip.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

A more sophisticated target-hardening approach must consider industrial and other critical industrial infrastructure that could be attacked. Terrorists do not need to import chemical weapons if they can cause a major industrial accident such as the [Bhopal disaster](/wiki/Bhopal_disaster "Bhopal disaster") or the [Halifax Explosion](/wiki/Halifax_Explosion "Halifax Explosion"). Industrial chemicals in manufacturing, shipping, and storage thus require greater protection, and some efforts are in progress.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Equipping likely targets with containers of pig lard has been used to discourage attacks by suicide bombers. The technique was apparently used on a limited scale by British authorities in the 1940s. The approach stems from the idea that Muslims perpetrating the attack would not want to be "soiled" by the lard in the moment before dying.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The idea has been suggested more recently as a deterrent to suicide bombings in Israel.[[41]](#cite_note-41) However, the actual effectiveness of this tactic is likely limited. A sympathetic Islamic scholar could issue a [fatwa](/wiki/Fatwa "Fatwa") proclaiming that a suicide bomber would not be polluted by the swine products.

### Command and control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Command and control")]

For a threatened or completed terrorist attack, an [Incident Command System](/wiki/Incident_Command_System "Incident Command System") (ICS) may be invoked to control the various services that may need to be involved in the response. ICS has varied levels of escalation, such as might be required for multiple incidents in a given area (e.g. [2005 London bombings](/wiki/7_July_2005_London_bombings "7 July 2005 London bombings") or the [2004 Madrid train bombings](/wiki/2004_Madrid_train_bombings "2004 Madrid train bombings")), or all the way to a [National Response Plan](/wiki/National_Response_Plan "National Response Plan") invocation if national-level resources are needed. For example, a national response might be required for a nuclear, biological, radiological, or significant chemical attack.

### Damage mitigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Damage mitigation")]

[Fire departments](/wiki/Fire_department "Fire department"), perhaps supplemented by public works agencies, utility providers, and heavy construction contractors, are most apt to deal with the physical consequences of an attack.

### Local security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Local security")]

Again under an incident command model, local police can isolate the incident area, reducing confusion, and specialized police units can conduct tactical operations against terrorists, often using specialized counterterrorist tactical units. Bringing in such units will typically involve civil or military authority beyond the local level.

### Medical services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Medical services")]

[Emergency medical services](/wiki/Emergency_medical_services "Emergency medical services") are capable of triaging, treating, and transporting the more severely affected individuals to hospitals, which typically have [mass casualty](/wiki/Mass-casualty_incident "Mass-casualty incident") and [triage](/wiki/Triage "Triage") plans in place for terrorist attacks.

From the local to the national level, [public health agencies](/wiki/Public_health_surveillance "Public health surveillance") may be designated to deal with identification or mitigation of possible biological attacks, including chemical or radiological contamination.

## Tactical units

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Tactical units")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F054217-0020%2C_Bundesgrenzschutz%2C_GSG_9.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F054217-0020%2C_Bundesgrenzschutz%2C_GSG_9.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F054217-0020,_Bundesgrenzschutz,_GSG_9.jpg)

A [GSG 9](/wiki/GSG_9 "GSG 9") operator during a 1978 training assault. Established in 1972, GSG 9 became one of the earliest dedicated counterterrorism tactical units

### Counterterrorism agencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Counterterrorism agencies")]

Main article: [List of counter-terrorism agencies](/wiki/List_of_counter-terrorism_agencies "List of counter-terrorism agencies")

Many countries have dedicated counterterrorist units trained to handle terrorist threats. Besides various [security agencies](/wiki/Security_agency "Security agency"), there are [police tactical units](/wiki/Police_tactical_unit "Police tactical unit") whose role is to directly engage terrorists and prevent terrorist attacks. Such units perform both in preventive actions, hostage rescue, and responding to ongoing attacks. Countries of all sizes can have highly trained counterterrorist teams. Tactics, techniques, and procedures for [manhunting](/wiki/Manhunt_(military) "Manhunt (military)") are under constant development.

These units are specially trained in [military tactics](/wiki/Military_tactics "Military tactics") and are equipped for [close-quarters combat](/wiki/Close-quarters_combat "Close-quarters combat"), with emphasis on stealth and performing the mission with minimal casualties. The units include assault teams, [snipers](/wiki/Sniper "Sniper"), [EOD](/wiki/Bomb_disposal "Bomb disposal") experts, dog handlers, and intelligence officers. Most of these measures deal with terrorist attacks that affect an area or threaten to do so, or are lengthy situations such as [shootouts](/wiki/Shootout "Shootout") and [standoffs](/wiki/Police_standoff "Police standoff") that allow the counterterrorist units to assemble and respond; it is harder to deal with shorter incidents such as assassinations, reprisal attacks, or bombings due to the short warning time and the quick exfiltration of the perpetrators.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The majority of counterterrorism operations at the tactical level are conducted by state, federal, and national law enforcement or [intelligence agencies](/wiki/Intelligence_agency "Intelligence agency"). In some countries, the military may be called in as a last resort. For countries whose military is legally permitted to conduct domestic law enforcement operations, this is not an issue, and such counterterrorism operations are conducted by their military.

### Designing counterterrorist systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Designing counterterrorist systems")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section's **tone or style may not reflect the [encyclopedic tone](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Tone "Wikipedia:Writing better articles") used on Wikipedia**. See Wikipedia's [guide to writing better articles](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Tone "Wikipedia:Writing better articles") for suggestions. *(June 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The scope for counterterrorism systems is very large in physical terms and in other dimensions, such as type and degree of terrorist threats, political and diplomatic ramifications, and legal concerns. Ideal counterterrorist systems use technology to enable persistent [intelligence](/wiki/Intelligence "Intelligence"), surveillance and reconnaissance missions, and potential actions. Designing such a system-of-systems comprises a major technological project.[[46]](#cite_note-:0-46)

A particular design problem for counterterrorist systems is the uncertainty of the future: the threat of terrorism may increase, decrease or remain the same, the type of terrorism and location are difficult to predict, and there are technological uncertainties. A potential solution is to incorporate [engineering flexibility](/wiki/Flexibility_(engineering) "Flexibility (engineering)") into system design, allowing for flexibility when new information arrives. Flexibility can be incorporated in the design of a counter-terrorism system in the form of options that can be exercised in the future when new information is available.[[46]](#cite_note-:0-46)

### Law enforcement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Law enforcement")]

While some countries with longstanding terrorism problems have law enforcement agencies primarily designed to prevent and respond to terror attacks,[[47]](#cite_note-47) in other nations, counterterrorism is a relatively more recent objective of law enforcement agencies.[[48]](#cite_note-Deflem-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Though some [civil libertarians](/wiki/Civil_libertarianism "Civil libertarianism") and [criminal justice](/wiki/Criminal_justice "Criminal justice") scholars have criticized efforts of law enforcement agencies to combat terrorism as futile and expensive[[50]](#cite_note-HelmsCostanzaJohnson-50) or as threats to civil liberties,[[50]](#cite_note-HelmsCostanzaJohnson-50) other scholars have analyzed the most important dimensions of the policing of terrorism as an important dimension of counter-terrorism, especially in the post-9/11 era, and have discussed how police view terrorism as a matter of crime control.[[48]](#cite_note-Deflem-48) Such analyses highlight the civilian police role in counterterrorism next to the military model of a war on terror.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

#### American law enforcement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: American law enforcement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1e/FBI_Hostage_Rescue_Team_Agents_bei_einer_%C3%9Cbung.jpg)](/wiki/File:FBI_Hostage_Rescue_Team_Agents_bei_einer_%C3%9Cbung.jpg)

[FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") [Hostage Rescue Team](/wiki/Hostage_Rescue_Team "Hostage Rescue Team") agents

Pursuant to passage of the [Homeland Security Act of 2002](/wiki/Homeland_Security_Act_of_2002 "Homeland Security Act of 2002"), federal, state, and local law enforcement agencies began to systemically reorganize.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) Two primary federal agencies, the [Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") (DOJ) and the [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/Department_of_Homeland_Security "Department of Homeland Security") (DHS), house most of the federal agencies that are prepared to combat domestic and international terrorist attacks. These include the [Border Patrol](/wiki/United_States_Border_Patrol "United States Border Patrol"), the [Secret Service](/wiki/United_States_Secret_Service "United States Secret Service"), the [Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard") and the [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI").

Following suit from federal changes pursuant to 9/11, however, most state and local law enforcement agencies began to include a commitment to "fighting terrorism" in their mission statements.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Local agencies began to establish more patterned lines of communication with federal agencies. Some scholars have doubted the ability of local police to help in the war on terror and suggest their limited manpower is still best utilized by engaging community and targeting street crimes.[[50]](#cite_note-HelmsCostanzaJohnson-50)

While counter-terror measures (most notably heightened airport security, immigrant [profiling](/wiki/Racial_profiling "Racial profiling")[[56]](#cite_note-56) and border patrol) have been adapted during the last decade, to enhance counter-terror in law enforcement, there have been remarkable limitations to assessing the actual utility/effectiveness of law enforcement practices that are ostensibly preventative.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Thus, while sweeping changes in counterterrorist rhetoric redefined most American post 9/11 law enforcement agencies in theory, it is hard to assess how well such hyperbole has translated into practice.

In [intelligence-led policing](/wiki/Intelligence-led_policing "Intelligence-led policing") (ILP) efforts, the most quantitatively amenable starting point for measuring the effectiveness of any policing strategy (i.e.: Neighborhood Watch, Gun Abatement, Foot Patrols, etc.) is usually to assess total financial costs against clearance rates or arrest rates. Since terrorism is such a rare event phenomena,[[58]](#cite_note-58) measuring arrests or clearance rates would be a non-generalizable and ineffective way to test enforcement policy effectiveness. Another methodological problem in assessing counterterrorism efforts in law enforcement hinges on finding operational measures for key concepts in the study of [homeland security](/wiki/Homeland_security "Homeland security"). Both terrorism and homeland security are relatively new concepts for criminologists, and academicians have yet to agree on the matter of how to properly define these ideas in a way that is accessible.

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counterterrorism&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Military")]

Further information: [List of special forces units](/wiki/List_of_special_forces_units "List of special forces units")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/DSA_2016_-_Breach_the_hideout.jpg/250px-DSA_2016_-_Breach_the_hideout.jpg)](/wiki/File:DSA_2016_-_Breach_the_hideout.jpg)

[Malaysian Army](/wiki/Malaysian_Army "Malaysian Army") [*Gerak Khas*](/wiki/Gerak_Khas_(Malaysian_Army_unit) "Gerak Khas (Malaysian Army unit)") operatives during a [training exercise](/wiki/Training_exercise "Training exercise") in 2016

Given the nature of operational counterterrorism tasks, national military organizations do not generally have dedicated units whose sole responsibility is the prosecution of these tasks. Instead the counterterrorism function is an element of the role, allowing flexibility in their employment, with operations being undertaken in the domestic or international context.

In some cases the legal framework within which they operate prohibits military units conducting operations in the domestic arena; [United States Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") policy, based on the [Posse Comitatus Act](/wiki/Posse_Comitatus_Act "Posse Comitatus Act"), forbids domestic counterterrorism operations by the U.S. military. Units allocated some operational counterterrorism tasks are frequently [special forces](/wiki/Special_forces "Special forces") or similar assets.

In cases where military organisations do operate in the domestic context, some form of formal handover from the law enforcement community is regularly required, to ensure adherence to the legislative framework and limitations. For instance, during the 1980 [Iranian Embassy siege](/wiki/Iranian_Embassy_siege "Iranian Embassy siege"), the [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") formally turned responsibility over to the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army")'s [Special Air Service](/wiki/Special_Air_Service "Special Air Service") when the situation went beyond police capabilities.
